frontmatter.title: 0: Prerequisites
frontmatter.summary: Foundational prerequisites for the Iliad Intensive — alignment background, technical knowledge, and engineering skills.

title: A.2: Overview of Deep Learning Theory 1

frontmatter.title: A.2: Overview of Deep Learning Theory 1
frontmatter.summary: First overview of deep learning theory — approximation, generalization, and optimization in over-parameterized neural networks.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
First, start with the lecture, which should take 1-1.5 hours. Ask for questions and discuss.

For readings:

- Read each piece one at a time, set a timer for how long you expect the reading to take (underestimate is good), and assess people's progress when the timer runs out and possibly/likely run the timer again. Then discuss in small groups (~4 people) for 10-30 mins, depending on the importance of the content and how much there is to digest. During small group discussions, you can walk around different groups and participate in different discussions
- In the case where the piece is too long to digest comfortably in a single setting, or is very dense, break it down into smaller chunks of 20-40 mins, with intermediate discussions. For A.2, this should only be the case for the Solomonoff induction reading (Shane Legg's thesis)

For exercises:

- Break the exercises into chunks depending on content and duration (aim for 20-40 mins). Set a timer for how long you expect the chunk to take (underestimate is good), and assess people's progress when the timer runs out and possibly/likely run the timer again. Explicitly tell the students they can ask for help at any time. After the chunk is up, ask questions of the class ("what answer did you get here? what pattern might that suggest?" etc), make some remarks about takeaways, etc. Repeat.

Throughout: ensure to take breaks regularly, every hour or two for at least 10-20 mins, and also the lunch break.

Only one TA is strictly required, though more can assist with facilitating discussions and exercises.
learningOutcomes: Students can explain the three fundamental barriers to building a universal learning machine: approximation, generalization, and optimization, and articulate why they are sometimes/naively in tension with each other

Students can explain what Solomonoff induction is, how it resolves the approximation and generalization barriers via a universal hypothesis class and simplicity prior, and why it fails on optimization
Students are aware of the optimality result for Solomonoff induction
Students can explain the bias-variance tradeoff and why it might suggest universal learning is impossible, and how the simplicity prior in Solomonoff induction sidesteps this
Students can state the no free lunch theorem and explain what it does and does not imply about practical learning
Students can explain, at a high level, why even polynomial-time hypothesis classes can be hard to learn over efficiently (the PRG/cryptographic hardness argument)
Students can articulate why learning requires structure in the world, and what this implies about what we should expect from powerful learning machines

Students understand the relevance of this material to AGI safety: if AGI is a learning machine, the three barriers constrain what it can look like, and Solomonoff induction serves as a theoretical reference point for reasoning about its capabilities
frontmatter.notesForFutureIterations:
- Making a comprehensive set of lecture notes that actually ties the readings together rather than just listing them would be nice. Or at a minimum just an annotated reading list
- Mathematically showing the exact decomposition of empirical risk into approximation, generalization, optimization in e.g. the ERM framework would be helpful, to make these categories less hand-wave-y
- Approximation theory and the curse of dimensionality should be covered today (e.g. the parameter count lower bound from [here](https://gwern.net/doc/cs/algorithm/1989-devore.pdf)), I just couldn't find any good existing resources so this might need to be created
- This covered a lot of ground in one day and people felt a bit rushed
- Preparing explicit discussion prompts ahead of time for readings could be a good idea
- Small group discussions (~4 people) generally work better than full class discussions
- Learning goals should be made more clear. People generally did not realize that the end goal here was *awareness* of these broader learning theory
- Spend more time talking about why this is useful for alignment
- People's backgrounds are very different, and some people will read 2x slower than others - this can sometimes mean a tradeoff between letting people slip behind or boring the most capable students. Not sure how to handle
- I (Zach Furman) am not an expert in Solomonoff induction but nevertheless ended up having to cover it, and my lack of knowledge was noticeable. Would have gone worse without other TAs there who knew more

title: A.3: Overview of Deep Learning Theory 2

frontmatter.title: A.3: Overview of Deep Learning Theory 2
frontmatter.summary: Second overview of deep learning theory — simplicity bias, mysteries of generalization, and connections to alignment.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
First, start with the lecture, which should take ~1 hour. Ask for questions and discuss.

For readings:

- Read each piece one at a time, set a timer for how long you expect the reading to take (underestimate is good), and assess people's progress when the timer runs out and possibly/likely run the timer again. Then discuss in small groups (~4 people) for 10-30 mins, depending on the importance of the content and how much there is to digest. During small group discussions, you can walk around different groups and participate in different discussions
- In the case where the piece is too long to digest comfortably in a single setting, or is very dense, break it down into smaller chunks of 20-40 mins, with intermediate discussions. This is especially true for the "Deep Learning as Program Synthesis" piece but also several others
- There is not enough time to read every piece, and so only some will have to be selected, possibly based on audience interest. It is important that students read "Deep Learning as Program Synthesis" (despite its length) as it covers most of these mysteries at once in a unified way, but the other readings are more for deepening understanding. In the April 2026 iteration, we read: "Deep Learning is Not So Mysterious or Different," "The Platonic Representation Hypothesis," and "In-context Learning and Induction Heads"
- For the later pieces it can help to explicitly encourage students to skim for high-level awareness/ideas rather than read closely, including by setting a short reading timer (relative to the length of the piece) that explicitly forces skimming
  - Note however that the two pieces listed under "optimization" do require deep engagement to get much value at all out of them, and likely cannot be skimmed productively

Throughout: ensure to take breaks regularly, every hour or two for at least 10-20 mins, and also the lunch break.

Only one TA is strictly required, though more can assist with facilitating discussions.
learningOutcomes: Students can explain how the three barriers from Day A.2 (approximation, generalization, optimization) manifest specifically in the context of deep learning, and why classical theoretical frameworks (e.g. bias-variance tradeoff, universal approximation) actively counterpredicted deep learning's success

Students are aware of the key empirical mysteries of deep learning: data-dependent generalization despite overparameterization, effectiveness of SGD on non-convex landscapes, representational alignment across architectures, and in-context learning
Students have encountered at least one candidate explanation for each mystery and can articulate what it does and doesn't explain
Students understand the "program synthesis" hypothesis as one proposed framework connecting deep learning to Solomonoff induction, and can evaluate its strengths and limitations

Students can articulate why solving these mysteries matters for AI safety: understanding the basic mechanisms by which deep learning works is necessary for any *systematic* (generalizing OOD) alignment interventions or measurements to even be possible

As with Day A.2, the goal is awareness and broad context, not deep technical mastery. The subsequent days (SLT, training dynamics, influence functions) go deep on specific approaches; this day ensures students know what those approaches are trying to explain

The reading list is deliberately longer than can be comfortably covered in one day. The TA should select a subset based on audience interest and pacing. The "Deep Learning as Program Synthesis" post is the one reading that should not be cut, as it provides the overarching framework and connects back to Day A.2
This day is reading-and-discussion-heavy rather than exercise-heavy. That's appropriate given the survey nature of the content, but it means discussion quality matters a lot
frontmatter.notesForFutureIterations:
- See the notes from Day A.2, many apply here as well
- Annotating the reading list here could be helpful
- Possibly the reading list should be curated down to a smaller list explicitly, as there is not enough time for even half of the readings to be covered

frontmatter.title: A.4: Singular Learning Theory
frontmatter.summary: Singular Learning Theory: degeneracy, the local learning coefficient, free energy, and Bayesian phase transitions.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
**Day plan** (ILO = Intended learning outcome; exercises with * = "if time allows"; all exercise numbers are from the "Spring 2026 pilot" version of the notes).
TimeTopicILOs
10:00– Block 1: Introduction to singular learning theory
Lecture: The puzzle of implicit model selection; preliminaries;
   defining degeneracy; local learning coefficient definition, intuitions;
   Bayesian learning; free energy formula; Bayesian phase
   transitions; resolving the puzzle; empirical work. 		1,2,3
10:30–
11:00– Block 2: What is degeneracy?
Notes: Sections 1.1, 1.2, 2.
Exercises:
1. Definition of degeneracy (ex. 2.1, 2.2, 2.3*);
2. Examples from deep learning (ex. 2.6*, 2.9, 2.10*);
3. Relating different definitions of degeneracy (ex. 2.12*, 2.15). 		1
11:30–
12:00–
12:30– Lunch break
13:00–
13:30– Block 3: The degeneracy hierarchy
Notes: Section 3.
Exercises:
1. Volume scaling (ex. 3.1, 3.2);
2. Learning coefficient via volume scaling (ex. 3.4, 3.5*);
3. Learning coefficient examples (ex. 3.7, 3.10). 		2
14:00–
14:30–
15:00–
15:30– Afternoon break
16:00– Block 4: Degeneracy and Bayesian deep learning
Notes: Sections 1.3, 1.4, 4.
Exercises:
1. Bayesian inference (ex. 1.3*, 1.7, 1.8);
2. Free energy formula, Bayesian phase transitions (ex. 4.2*). 		3
16:30–
17:00–
17:30– Block 5: Closing remarks
Lecture: Summary; looking ahead; where to find further resources
   and get involved ("learn more" content).
Feedback survey (10 minutes) 		N/A
18:00 End of day
The main format of each block will be either a lecture or an exercise session. - A lecture block is as you'd expect, Zach delivered the lecture slides, sometimes pausing for student questions. The goal was to frame the whole day. We would revisit the themes from the lecture throughout the exercise blocks. - The exercise blocks worked as follows. - TAs would start by giving a brief (10 min) oral/whiteboard overview of the theme for the block and directing students to the relevant section of the notes. - Then we set them the first exercise(s), give them 20 minutes or so to work on it. TAs walk around and help anyone who needs assistance with the exercises. - Once everyone is done, give a brief (5–10 min) oral/whiteboard recap of the solution/takeaways, and set the next exercise(s). Repeat until the end of the block. There should be maybe one TA per ~10 students, as long as they are fully available (e.g. not working on their own stuff or in another room, available to help students on demand).
learningOutcomes: Define degeneracy of parameter–function maps and identify degenerate directions in simple models (including deep linear networks and multi-layer perceptrons).
Define the local learning coefficient via volume scaling asymptotics, compute learning coefficients in simple examples, and compare to the regular and minimally singular cases.
State Watanabe's free energy formula and analyse toy examples of Bayesian phase transitions.
frontmatter.notesForFutureIterations:
### Retrospective

The teaching plan section is an up-to-date summary that reflects what we actually got time to cover and what the exercise sessions looked like.

Exercise session notes:

- MFR: One thing we could have done better was encourage students to work in groups, the room was mostly silent when nobody was asking questions.
- MFR: Another thing we could have done better was encourage students to consult AIs if they need a primer on some context (or otherwise), though TAs were also available in this case.
- MFR: Another thing we could have done is encourage strong students to work ahead if they finish early.

Feedback was mostly positive:

- Several people commented on lecture notes and exercises being good.
  - Nice quote: "Initial lecture, exercise and discussion format - all very good! (And this presentation is massively better than what was available online around 2 years ago)"
- Several people commented on liking the format, as a mix between lecture, exercises, discussion.

Some things mentioned as areas we could improve (see the feedback form for full quotes):

- Visualisations
  - We have noted this below
- Spend more time on preliminaries early in the day
  - We assigned this as pre-reading, but could also consider allocating more time
- More links with actual AI safety stuff; links to core issues in alignment.
  - We did try to connect this in the wrap-up discussion at the end of the day
  - Probably more could be included in the framing lecture at the start of the day
- We should make clearer
  - Already noted above, and below we suggest using ARENA-style time/difficulty indicators on the exercises
- Looking for more challenging exercises later in the day
  - Perhaps they mean section 4, agree we should add more + deeper exercises.
- "I want to read counterargument or adjacent-varient theories to know how confident I should be about the likelihood of this theory and how strongly I should pursue it."
  - This is what the other days are for…!
- Too much time on math proof / mathematical, wants us to cater more to people with different backgrounds.
  - This is intentional at the level of Iliad Intensive, don't think we need to do anything.
- Someone said it was all too fast, they couldn't keep up and wished they could have just worked at their own pace
  - So the good news is that the lecture notes support this person working through at their own pace.
  - Since only a couple of people expressed concerns like this, probably we are going at about the right pace overall.
  - Potentially the cause here is that this person was not comfortable with the preliminaries sections that we assigned as pre-readings. It would be good if they had found time to do the pre-readings, but also we could potentially try to make space for pre-readings in the first exercise block.

In-person feedback:

- Vaguely positive
- Nothing specific to note.

### Future improvements

"If you or someone else developed this module section further, what should they change?"

- MFR: I think I'm pretty happy with it for one day. See answers below.
- MFR: The notes themselves I have various plans for improvement, not specific enough to write down yet though. Could basically put in about as much effort as before to make a more expanded and thorough version of the notes.

"What content could be added?"

- MFR: There is already enough for one day, mostly should focus on delivering that content better.
- MFR: If you want extra SLT days, could have:
  - For a similar audience of mostly physicists, could have a day on Bayesian interp foundations, such as LLC estimator, susceptibilities and their estimation.
  - For an audience of scientists/empiricists, a day on the empirical science/interp work discussed in the further readings section of the notes.
  - For a more mathematically mature audience, could have a day doing a deep dive into the underlying algebraic geometry and Bayesian theory.
- ZF: Add images/figures (consider using existing ones from Timaeus blog posts / publications).
  - ZF: Can also leverage exercises based on Desmos, etc
- MFR: Flesh out section 4 (on Bayesian phase transitions) with more / deeper exercises.
  - Perhaps it would be possible to add an example of a Bayesian phase transition in a DLN, if we can find a clean one.
- MFR: Would be nice to add an exercise like the DLN LLC one, but on tanh networks, paralleling how we have both DLN and tanh networks int he "what is degeneracy?" section.

"What could be explained better with more effort?"

- MFR: I think maybe we haven't found the right way to frame the distinction between degeneracy in the loss vs. in the parameter–function map.
  - ZF: I think the Hessian chain rule calculation is fairly enlightening but elementary (doesn't require the FIM, etc)
    - MFR: This could be added as an exercise.
- KO: Maybe more attention to preliminaries (section 1), which we set as pre-reading, but not everyone was on board with this.
- KO: Since some parts of the notes are skipped during the day, make sure the different sections in the day are motivated and linked to each other. There is a chance of accidentally skipping small but important details when skipping parts of the notes.

"How could the content be made more scalable? More self-contained?"

- MFR: I think having the notes already makes the content very self-contained, not much more is needed.
  - MFR/KO: Perhaps some solutions could be improved, made clearer.
  - Exercises could be more battle-tested and refined with student experience (add appropriate hints, clear up confusing wording, etc.)
- KO: The ARENA exercises have ratings for difficulty, importance and expected duration, providing this kind of indicator could help students self-study the material, e.g. know when to consult solution or LLM.

"Is there some research project which, if finished, would allow a more streamlined explanation of this topic?"

- MFR: General progress in theoretical SLT, e.g. progress on SGD theory, could warrant updating the presentation. Absent that, I think the notes are a pretty good first day introduction to the current framework in the field.
